topic.title: HTML Image Performance and Branding
topic.description: loading=lazy reduces page weight for off-screen images; favicon links brand tabs and home screens.

content.text: Image Performance and Branding: loading, decoding, and favicon

text.value: Two distinct concerns sit at the intersection of images and site quality. The first is
: images are the largest contributors to page weight and
attribute defers fetching off-screen images until the user scrolls near them, reducing initial page load cost. The
attribute lets the browser decode image data off the main thread. The
attribute elevates the network priority of the LCP image. The second concern is
specifies the favicon displayed in browser tabs, bookmarks, and app icons. Modern favicon implementation requires multiple
declarations covering SVG, PNG at multiple sizes, Apple Touch icon, and a Web App Manifest entry. Reference:

rows: Defer image fetch until near viewport
Raise network priority for one image
Required for PWA and iOS sharing
Web App Manifest with icons array

content.title: Never Lazy-Load the LCP Image
content.text: The Largest Contentful Paint image is the most important image on the page for Core Web Vitals. Adding loading='lazy' to the LCP image delays its network request until JavaScript has run and intersection observers have fired, which significantly degrades LCP scores. The LCP image should always use loading='eager' (or omit the attribute, since eager is the default) and should use fetchpriority='high' to raise its priority in the browser's preload scanner.

examples.title: Lazy Loading, fetchpriority, and decoding Attributes
examples.description: This example builds a blog post page with a hero image using fetchpriority='high', a grid of article cards with lazy-loaded images, the decoding='async' pattern for large decorative images, and a native browser lazy loading demo. It shows the complete attribute set for both above-fold and below-fold images.
examples.code_snippet:
HTML Foundations Course

12 modules · 48 lessons · Certificate on completion

Recent Articles

Understanding the CSS Cascade

How browsers decide which rule wins

A Complete Guide to Flexbox

Every property explained with examples

CSS Grid Layout in Depth

Two-dimensional layouts made simple

HTML Accessibility Checklist

WCAG 2.1 AA for everyday developers

Image loading attribute decision guide

Rule 1: First image in viewport (LCP candidate)
loading="eager" fetchpriority="high" decoding="async"

Rule 2: Above-fold images (not the LCP image)
loading="eager" (default; omit fetchpriority)

Rule 3: Below-fold images (all article cards, gallery items)
loading="lazy" decoding="async"

Rule 4: All images, always
width and height attributes to prevent CLS

Common loading attribute mistakes

Wrong: lazy on the LCP image

<img src="hero.jpg" loading="lazy" fetchpriority="high">
loading="lazy" delays the request; fetchpriority="high" is wasted.

Right: eager + high priority for LCP

<img src="hero.jpg" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high">

Wrong: missing width/height on lazy image

<img src="card.jpg" loading="lazy">
No dimensions = layout shift when image loads.

Right: lazy with explicit dimensions

<img src="card.jpg" loading="lazy" width="440" height="280">
examples.output: Page renders a hero section with a full-width image overlaid with a course title. Below it, a four-column card grid shows article thumbnails with lazy-loaded images. The annotation section shows the four loading rules as a quick-reference guide. The wrong versus right section shows four code comparisons: lazy on LCP image (wrong) versus eager plus high priority (right), and missing dimensions (wrong) versus explicit width and height (right).

examples.title: Complete Favicon Implementation for All Platforms
examples.description: This example shows the full modern favicon head block: an SVG icon with light/dark mode support, a PNG fallback at 32x32, an Apple Touch icon at 180x180, a Web App Manifest reference, and a browserconfig.xml reference for legacy Windows tiles. It also shows the SVG favicon with prefers-color-scheme media query.
examples.code_snippet:
Favicon Implementation Reference

1. SVG favicon with dark mode

Save as /favicon.svg. The prefers-color-scheme media query inside the SVG switches the icon colour automatically.

<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 32 32">
  <!-- Default (light mode): dark icon -->
  <style>
    .icon-bg   { fill: #2563eb; }
    .icon-text { fill: #ffffff; font-family: system-ui, sans-serif;
                 font-weight: 700; }
    @media (prefers-color-scheme: dark) {
      /* Dark mode: white background, blue text */
      .icon-bg   { fill: #ffffff; }
      .icon-text { fill: #2563eb; }
    }
  </style>
  <rect class="icon-bg" width="32" height="32" rx="6"/>
  <text class="icon-text" x="16" y="22"
        text-anchor="middle" font-size="18">D</text>
</svg>

2. site.webmanifest

Save as /site.webmanifest. This JSON file provides icons for Android and PWA install prompts.

{
  "name": "DevDocs India",
  "short_name": "DevDocs",
  "description": "HTML, CSS, and JavaScript learning platform",
  "start_url": "/",
  "display": "standalone",
  "background_color": "#ffffff",
  "theme_color": "#2563eb",
  "icons": [
    {
      "src": "/icons/icon-192.png",
      "sizes": "192x192",
      "type": "image/png",
      "purpose": "any maskable"
    },
    {
      "src": "/icons/icon-512.png",
      "sizes": "512x512",
      "type": "image/png",
      "purpose": "any maskable"
    }
  ]
}

3. Favicon file checklist

File Size Format Used by
favicon.svg Scalable SVG Chrome, Firefox, Edge (modern)
favicon-32.png 32 x 32 px PNG Older browsers, PNG fallback
apple-touch-icon.png 180 x 180 px PNG iOS/iPadOS home screen
icon-192.png 192 x 192 px PNG Android home screen, PWA
icon-512.png 512 x 512 px PNG PWA splash screen, app store

4. Legacy favicon.ico (optional)

Place a favicon.ico file at the root of the domain (/favicon.ico). Browsers request this path automatically as a fallback even without a <link> tag. A multi-resolution ICO file contains 16x16 and 32x32 copies. Only needed if supporting Internet Explorer or very old browsers.

<!-- Only add this if you need IE11 support -->
<link rel="shortcut icon" href="/favicon.ico" type="image/x-icon">

<!-- Modern equivalent (use instead of shortcut icon) -->
<link rel="icon" href="/favicon.ico" sizes="16x16 32x32" type="image/x-icon">
examples.output: Page renders a favicon implementation reference with four sections. The SVG favicon section shows a dark code block with the complete SVG markup including an embedded style block with prefers-color-scheme dark mode switch. The manifest section shows the site.webmanifest JSON with name, icons array at 192 and 512, and purpose fields. The file checklist table lists all five required files with sizes, formats, and which platforms use each. The legacy section explains favicon.ico placement and shows the modern versus legacy link element syntax.

on exactly one image per page: the
, which is typically the hero image or the largest above-fold image. Elevating multiple images wastes the priority signal.

Implement the full favicon set (SVG + PNG-32 + Apple Touch + manifest) for every production site to ensure correct icon display across browser tabs, bookmarks, iOS home screens, and Android PWA install prompts.

to more than one image per page; the signal loses meaning when multiple images compete for elevated priority and the browser falls back to its own heuristics.
for modern sites; ICO files do not support SVG scalability, dark mode, or the sizes required by iOS and Android home screen icons.
polyfills with a single HTML attribute, reducing initial page weight and time-to-interactive without any script dependency.
: the browser's speculative fetcher treats all images equally until this attribute signals which single image is the most important to the page's perceived load time.
: a single ICO or PNG appears blurry on Retina displays, missing on iOS home screens, and absent from PWA install prompts. The SVG + manifest approach covers all surfaces with one source file.
Native lazy loading works through the browser's
, not JavaScript. When the parser encounters
, it records the image URL but does not add it to the fetch queue. An internal

-equivalent mechanism triggers the fetch when the image enters a distance threshold from the viewport (typically 1200px on fast connections, closer on slow ones). This means lazy loading works even when JavaScript is disabled.
is a secondary HTML parser that runs ahead of the main parser, scanning for

tells this scanner to give the LCP image a higher queue position than other images. Without it, the hero image competes equally with all other images discovered at parse time, delaying the most important paint.
attribute. The browser picks the first

it can render: SVG if supported, then PNG, then ICO. Declaring them in capability order (SVG first, ICO last) ensures each browser uses the best format it understands without JavaScript feature detection.
on the LCP hero image delays its fetch and worsens
on lazy images causes layout shift when they load and push content down.
without an Apple Touch icon or manifest leaves iOS and PWA icons missing.
for the LCP image when it is discovered late (inside CSS
or JavaScript). Preload pulls the image into the fetch queue before the parser or preload scanner would normally discover it, improving LCP for dynamically rendered hero images.
Generate the full favicon set from a single high-resolution source using

block. Starting from a 512x512 PNG or SVG produces the sharpest results at all target sizes.

subtopics.title: Lazy Loading Images
subtopics.description: loading='lazy' defers image fetches until the image nears the viewport, cutting initial page weight.

text.value: controls when the browser initiates the fetch.
defers the request until the element is within a browser-defined distance from the viewport.
attribute controls whether image decoding blocks the main thread:
(default) lets the browser decide. Always provide
attributes on lazy images to allow the browser to reserve space and prevent

content.title: loading='lazy' Has No Effect Without Dimensions
content.text: When a lazy image lacks width and height attributes, the browser cannot compute the image's intrinsic size before the image loads. This means the browser may decide the image is already in the viewport (since its placeholder has zero height) and fetch it immediately, defeating the purpose of lazy loading. Always pair loading='lazy' with explicit width and height attributes or an aspect-ratio CSS rule.

examples.title: Lazy Loading with Dimensions, decoding, and fetchpriority
examples.description: This example shows the full attribute set for the three image scenarios: LCP hero image with eager and high priority, standard above-fold images with eager, and below-fold gallery images with lazy and async decoding. It includes a JavaScript IntersectionObserver fallback pattern for older browsers and a demonstration of width and height preventing layout shift.
examples.code_snippet:
Lazy Loading: loading, decoding, fetchpriority

1. LCP hero image (eager + high priority)

Attributes used:
loading="eager" — fetch immediately (critical)
fetchpriority="high" — highest network priority
decoding="async" — decode off main thread
width="720" height="405" — prevents CLS

2. Above-fold supporting images (eager, no special priority)

Priya Sharma is the lead instructor for the HTML and accessibility modules. She has taught web development to over 12,000 students across India and is a contributor to the W3C HTML specification.

3. Below-fold gallery images (lazy + async)

4. Common mistakes

Wrong: lazy on LCP image

<img src="hero.jpg" alt="..." loading="lazy">

Right: eager + fetchpriority on LCP image

<img src="hero.jpg" alt="..." loading="eager" fetchpriority="high">

Wrong: lazy image without dimensions

<img src="thumb.jpg" alt="..." loading="lazy">

Right: lazy image with width and height

<img src="thumb.jpg" alt="..." loading="lazy" width="240" height="160">

5. JavaScript fallback (for browsers without native lazy loading)

<!-- Use data-src instead of src for the fallback pattern -->
<img
  data-src="/img/course-html.jpg"
  src="/img/placeholder-1x1.gif"
  alt="HTML course thumbnail"
  width="240"
  height="160"
  class="lazyload">

<script>
  // Only run if loading attribute is not supported
  if ('loading' in HTMLImageElement.prototype) {
    // Native lazy loading: convert data-src to src
    document.querySelectorAll('img[data-src]').forEach(img => {
      img.src = img.dataset.src;
    });
  } else {
    // Polyfill: IntersectionObserver for older browsers
    const observer = new IntersectionObserver(entries => {
      entries.forEach(entry => {
        if (entry.isIntersecting) {
          const img = entry.target;
          img.src = img.dataset.src;
          observer.unobserve(img);
        }
      });
    }, { rootMargin: '200px' });

    document.querySelectorAll('img.lazyload').forEach(img => {
      observer.observe(img);
    });
  }
</script>
examples.output: Page renders five sections. The LCP hero image section shows a full-width image with an annotation card listing all four attributes. The above-fold section shows a circular instructor avatar floating left with wrapping text. The gallery section shows a six-image grid with lazy-loaded thumbnails. The common mistakes section shows four code cards alternating red wrong and green right. The JavaScript fallback section shows a dark code block with the feature detection pattern and IntersectionObserver polyfill.

is encountered, the parser marks the image as deferred and does not add it to the network fetch queue. The browser's internal viewport proximity algorithm determines when to actually fetch the image. This means lazy loading functions correctly in environments where JavaScript is slow to execute or disabled entirely, unlike
in the layout. The browser computes
as the intrinsic aspect ratio and applies it via the CSS

property automatically. Without these attributes, lazy images have zero height when deferred, the browser thinks they are already in the viewport, may fetch them immediately, and when they do load they cause

or a JavaScript-rendered component. The preload scanner cannot see CSS backgrounds or dynamically inserted images; an explicit
pushes the fetch into the queue as early as the browser parses
, matching the performance of a native

subtopics.title: Favicon Implementation
subtopics.description: Complete favicon setup requires SVG, PNG at 32px, Apple Touch icon, and a Web App Manifest entry.

text.value: provides a scalable icon with optional dark-mode support via an embedded
provides a PNG fallback for older browsers.
specifies the iOS home screen icon.
array covers Android and PWA install surfaces. Browsers resolve icon declarations in source order, using the first format they support. Reference:

examples.title: Full Favicon Head Block and SVG with Dark Mode
examples.description: This example shows the complete copy-paste favicon head block for a production site, the SVG favicon source with embedded dark mode media query, the site.webmanifest JSON, the full file checklist, and the legacy favicon.ico fallback pattern for older browser support.
examples.code_snippet:
Favicon Implementation Reference

1. favicon.svg with prefers-color-scheme

Place at /favicon.svg. The embedded style block switches icon colours automatically in dark mode.

<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 32 32">
  <style>
    .bg   { fill: #2563eb; }
    .glyph{ fill: #ffffff;
            font-family: system-ui, sans-serif;
            font-weight: 700; }
    @media (prefers-color-scheme: dark) {
      .bg    { fill: #ffffff; }
      .glyph { fill: #2563eb; }
    }
  </style>
  <rect class="bg" width="32" height="32" rx="6"/>
  <text class="glyph" x="16" y="22"
        text-anchor="middle" font-size="18">D</text>
</svg>

In light mode: blue square, white letter D.
In dark mode: white square, blue letter D.
No JavaScript required. No server-side logic required.

2. site.webmanifest

{
  "name": "DevDocs India",
  "short_name": "DevDocs",
  "description": "HTML, CSS, and JavaScript courses",
  "start_url": "/",
  "display": "standalone",
  "background_color": "#ffffff",
  "theme_color": "#2563eb",
  "icons": [
    {
      "src": "/icons/icon-192.png",
      "sizes": "192x192",
      "type": "image/png",
      "purpose": "any maskable"
    },
    {
      "src": "/icons/icon-512.png",
      "sizes": "512x512",
      "type": "image/png",
      "purpose": "any maskable"
    }
  ]
}

3. Required files and sizes

FileDimensionsFormatPlatform
favicon.svg ScalableSVG Chrome, Firefox, Edge (modern)
favicon-32.png 32 x 32PNG Older browsers, PNG fallback
apple-touch-icon.png 180 x 180PNG iOS and iPadOS home screen
icon-192.png 192 x 192PNG Android home screen, PWA
icon-512.png 512 x 512PNG PWA splash screen, app store

4. Legacy favicon.ico (IE11 and older)

<!-- Place a multi-resolution ICO at the root.
     Browsers auto-request /favicon.ico as a last resort. -->

<!-- Modern link element (use this) -->
<link rel="icon"
      href="/favicon.ico"
      sizes="16x16 32x32"
      type="image/x-icon">

<!-- Old shortcut icon syntax (avoid in new projects) -->
<link rel="shortcut icon" href="/favicon.ico">

5. Testing your favicon

  • Open the page in Chrome, Firefox, and Safari — check the tab icon.
  • Bookmark the page — check the bookmark icon.
  • On iOS: tap Share → Add to Home Screen — check the home icon.
  • Use RealFaviconGenerator checker for a full platform audit.
  • Open DevTools → Application → Manifest to inspect the manifest icons.
examples.output: Page renders five sections. The SVG favicon section shows a dark code block with the complete SVG including an embedded style element with prefers-color-scheme, and a text description of light versus dark mode appearance. The manifest section shows the complete JSON with two icon entries. The file checklist table lists all five files. The legacy section shows both modern and old-style ICO link syntax. The testing section shows a five-step checklist with a link to the RealFaviconGenerator checker.

text.value: , not source order. A browser that supports SVG favicons will use the SVG
it appears. A browser that only supports ICO will skip SVG and PNG declarations and use the ICO. Declaring all formats in
gives every browser the best format it can render without any JavaScript feature detection.

media query works because the browser renders the SVG into a bitmap for the tab icon. Since the browser applies its own light/dark mode when rendering SVG, the embedded CSS media query fires correctly. This means a single SVG file serves both light and dark mode users without requiring two separate
on both entries in the manifest
icons allow Android to apply platform-specific shape masks (circle, squircle, teardrop) to the icon. Without

, Android crops the icon in a white circle, often cutting off the logo. Design the icon with a safe zone: keep the main graphic inside the central 80% of the canvas to avoid clipping by any mask shape.

seo_metadata.meta_title: HTML Image Performance and Branding | Lazy Loading and Favicon Guide
seo_metadata.meta_description: Master HTML image performance with loading='lazy', fetchpriority, and decoding attributes, plus complete favicon implementation with SVG, PNG, Apple Touch, and Web App Manifest.
